answer:
**Manusmṛti (Mānava-Dharmaśāstra) 7.20** is a verse from Chapter 7 ("The Duties of a King") of the ancient Indic legal text attributed to Manu. It states:

> If the king did not, unwearied, apply punishment to those deserving punishment, the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit.[^1]

This verse is part of a broader discussion (primarily verses 7.14–30) on *daṇḍa* (Sanskrit: दण्ड, often translated as "punishment," "the rod," or "coercive force"), which is personified as the divine instrument of order created by the Creator for the king's use.[^2] 

*Daṇḍa* is depicted as the ultimate protector of Dharma: the king wields it to prevent chaos, ensuring weaker beings are not devoured by the strong (as in 7.20–21). Without its proper, discerning application—considering time, place, ability, and sacred law—society collapses into anarchy, with castes (*varṇas*) corrupted and natural hierarchies overturned.[^3] When wielded justly by a pure, wise king, *daṇḍa* upholds all beings' duties and brings prosperity; misused, it destroys the king and his realm.[^4]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, [7.20](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, [7.14](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, [7.15–16](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-55e886), [7.20–24](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^4]: Manusmṛti, [7.17–19](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c970b2), [7.26–27](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-96ec46).
